Metadata.Coram: Hector Finance Group Ltd and another v Chan Chew Keak [2023] SGHC 127
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hector Finance Group Limited

Facts.Text: Hector Finance Group Limited was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in May 2005 as a company established to invest in corrugated paper box plants producing container packaging in the People’s Republic of China and Southeast Asia.

Hector Finance Group Limited became the ultimate holding company of a group of companies that produce and sell paper products and container packaging in the People’s Republic of China and Southeast Asia.
Over 70% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited were owned and controlled by four individuals: Chan Chew Keak, Siong Beng Seng, Edward Ching Hui Huat, and Keith Tay Ah Kee.

Caldicott Worldwide Ltd, a British Virgin Islands company, owned 40% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited, and Chan Chew Keak owned 50% of the shares in Caldicott Worldwide Ltd, giving him an interest in at least 20% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited.

There was a suggestion within the group that Chan Chew Keak in fact owned all of the shares in Caldicott Worldwide Ltd, which would give him an interest in 40% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited.
Siong Beng Seng owned 23% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited.
Edward Ching Hui Huat owned 16% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited.

Springfield Investments & Nominees Pte Ltd, a Singapore company beneficially owned by Keith Tay Ah Kee, owned 14% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited, giving Keith Tay Ah Kee an interest in 14% of the shares.
Six minority shareholders collectively owned the remaining approximately 7% of the shares in Hector Finance Group Limited.
From its incorporation in 2005, Hector Finance Group Limited functioned as the holding company of a group of packaging businesses operating in the People’s Republic of China and Southeast Asia.

From 2005 until disputes arose in 2019, Chan Chew Keak internally used and presented the title of Chairman of Hector Finance Group Limited and of the group, although this title was not formally conferred by the company.

From 2005 until 2019, at a high level, strategic direction and overall management decisions for Hector Finance Group Limited and its group were given by Chan Chew Keak, with operational implementation carried out by senior managers including Siong Beng Seng and Edward Ching Hui Huat.

From 2005 to 2019, day-to-day management of Hector Finance Group Limited and the group was jointly handled by Siong Beng Seng and Edward Ching Hui Huat, while Keith Tay Ah Kee remained a non-operational investor.

From 2005 until 2019, internal management practice within Hector Finance Group Limited required that significant capital expenditure and major disposals of funds by group companies be brought to the board of Hector Finance Group Limited for approval.

From 2005 until 2019, internal practice within Hector Finance Group Limited was that major investment decisions for subsidiaries were discussed among the main shareholders and directors, including Chan Chew Keak, Siong Beng Seng, and Edward Ching Hui Huat.

From 2005 until 2019, Hector Finance Group Limited’s board-level decisions on group expansion and plant investments were typically implemented through its wholly owned Hong Kong subsidiary AMB Interpac Containers Guangdong Ltd and its downstream subsidiaries.
The defendant was a director of the first plaintiff from 2018 until he was removed from that position in 2020.
Following a February 2018 meeting, minutes were circulated recording a proposal for a sheet board plant, including an estimated funding requirement of around RMB 200m.
In July 2019, the first plaintiff's directors resolved that the Group should not proceed with any investment in a sheet board plant.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a holding company can establish a claim for breach of fiduciary duty or duty of diligence regarding funds advanced by a subsidiary to a third party when the holding company itself has not suffered a distinct financial loss.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for breach of fiduciary duty or diligence requires establishing a specific loss suffered by the claimant. Under the reflective loss principle, a shareholder or holding company generally cannot recover for a loss that is merely a reflection of a loss suffered by its subsidiary, particularly where the funds in question were advanced by the subsidiary rather than the holding company.
Party_Details.Application: Hector Finance Group Limited, as the ultimate holding company, maintained that its director breached duties regarding the deployment of RMB 14 million. However, the funds were advanced to the third party by its subsidiary, Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited, not by the holding company itself. While the holding company oversaw strategic group investments and internal approval processes, it did not demonstrate any loss to its own assets separate from the potential diminution in value of its shareholding in the subsidiary structure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A non-custodial breach of fiduciary duty and the duty of diligence are only actionable upon proof of loss to the specific claimant. Losses incurred at the subsidiary level cannot be transformed into losses of the holding company merely because of the group structure or the use of consolidated accounts. Because the intra-group transfers and the subsequent third-party loans involved the subsidiary's resources, any potential damage to the holding company is entirely reflective. In the absence of a distinct and pleaded loss to the holding company's own assets, the claims for breach of duty must be dismissed.

Metadata.Coram: Hector Finance Group Ltd and another v Chan Chew Keak [2023] SGHC 127
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited

Facts.Text: Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited was incorporated in the People’s Republic of China in August 2018 as a wholly owned subsidiary of AMB Interpac Containers (Guangdong) Ltd.
From its incorporation in August 2018 until 2019, Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited had Chan Chew Keak as its sole legal representative and director.
Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited was designated within the group to be the operating company for a proposed sheet board plant in the People’s Republic of China.
In January 2019, Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited received RMB 30 million from AMB Interpac Containers (Guangdong) Ltd.
In January 2019, the company entered into a written loan agreement with a third party, Li Yuanchang, for RMB 10 million with a two-year term and 3% interest.
Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited transferred RMB 10 million to Li Yuanchang in January 2019.
In March 2019, the company entered into a second written loan agreement with Li Yuanchang for RMB 4 million.
The company transferred RMB 4 million to Li Yuanchang on the same day as the second loan agreement.
By mid-2019, Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited and group personnel were unable to establish contact with Li Yuanchang.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches the duty of diligence under PRC Law by causing a company to advance substantial unsecured loans to a third-party consultant without proper board authorization or adequate contractual safeguards.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Articles 147 and 148 of the PRC Company Law, a director owes duties of fidelity and diligence to the company. The duty of diligence requires a director to act with the care that a reasonably competent administrator would exercise. In the absence of specific constitutional provisions, significant loans to third parties funded from corporate resources intended for capital projects require formal board or shareholder consent.
Party_Details.Application: Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited advanced a total of RMB 14 million to an individual designated as a land consultant. The loans were funded from intra-group transfers specifically meant for a plant development project. The director, acting as the company's sole legal representative, executed these loan agreements without seeking the formal consent of the holding company's board, despite internal practices requiring such approval for significant disposals of money. The loans were structured with minimal contractual protections and a low interest rate, and the individual subsequently became uncontactable, leading to the non-recovery of the principal sums.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director who causes a company to enter into substantial loan agreements must meet the objective standard of a reasonably competent administrator. While the objective of securing land for a plant is a legitimate business goal, the implementation through unsecured loans to a consultant without robust risk controls or formal board approval fails to meet the standard of diligence. The lack of documented internal consent for such significant capital deployment constitutes a breach of statutory duties. Consequently, the director is liable to compensate the company for the unrecovered principal and interest resulting from this lack of diligence.

Metadata.Coram: Hector Finance Group Ltd and another v Chan Chew Keak [2023] SGHC 127
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chan Chew Keak

Facts.Text: Chan Chew Keak acted as the de facto strategic leader of the Hector group, issuing directions on overall business strategy and investments.
Chan Chew Keak was a director of Hector Finance Group Limited and the sole director of Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited.
The individual proposed that the group invest in a new sheet board plant in the People’s Republic of China.

The individual emailed senior managers requesting that AMB Interpac Containers (Guangdong) Ltd transfer RMB 30 million to Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited for specified plant-related purposes.
As sole director, the individual caused Huizhou Xinsheng Paper Industry Co. Limited to transfer RMB 14 million to Li Yuanchang under two written loan agreements.
After the group decided not to proceed with the plant, the individual requested repayment of the loans, but the recipient was uncontactable.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director's unilateral decision to deploy company funds into third-party loans for a project, without formal board approval or adequate safeguards, constitutes a breach of the duty of diligence.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors are subject to an objective standard of care and must exercise the diligence of a reasonably competent administrator. This includes ensuring that substantial disposals of corporate funds are authorized by the appropriate governing bodies and are structured to protect the company's financial interests.
Party_Details.Application: Chan Chew Keak directed the transfer of RMB 30 million to a subsidiary and subsequently authorized RMB 14 million in loans to an individual acting as a land consultant. He relied on informal strategic discussions but did not obtain the formal board resolutions required for such significant capital expenditure. The loans lacked typical commercial protections, and he proceeded with the transfers despite the company's parlous financial exposure to the project. When the borrower defaulted and vanished, the funds remained unrecovered.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: An individual in a position of strategic leadership and statutory directorship must comply with internal standard operating procedures regarding the disposal of monies. Initiating substantial unsecured loans to a third party without formal board consent or rigorous risk management falls below the standard of diligence required by law. While the loans were intended to facilitate a business project, the manner of their execution was unreasonable for a competent administrator. The director's failure to adhere to procedural requirements and protect corporate assets from foreseeable risk establishes liability for the resulting loss. However, claims of a conspiracy to intentionally injure the company are not sustained where the primary motive was the pursuit of a business opportunity rather than malice.
